course_description:
This course provides a foundation for the next level of discipline-specific research methods by introducing qualitative and quantitative research methods, as well as a practical understanding of research in academic and professional settings.

This course introduces students to the communication research process. It aims to develop students’ ability to evaluate research and apply insights from research to address real world problems. The goal is for students to become educated consumers of research so that they can use research to inform their future professional practice. A ‘learning by doing’ approach is taken in the course to help students gain a deeper understanding of the research process. The course covers principles of qualitative and quantitative research, basic qualitative and quantitative analysis of data and commonly used qualitative and quantitative research methods. ﾠ

restrictions: Course offering may be cancelled unless a minimum of 35 students enrol.

timetable:
The timetable for this course is available on the
UQ Public Timetable

Additional timetable information
Whilst every effort is made to place students in their preferred activity, it is not always possible for a student to be enrolled in their tutorial of choice. If you require assistance, please ensure that you email
timetabling.commarts@enquire.uq.edu.au
from your
UQ student email
with:
Your name
Your student ID
The course code
A list of three tutorial preferences (in order of preference)
Reason for the change – e.g. timetable clash, elite athlete status, SAP
Teaching staff do not have access to change tutorials or help with timetables; all timetabling changes must be processed through the Timetabling Team.
course_aims: This course aims to develop students' understanding of the research process and their ability to evaluate and use research to inform real world practice. The course introduces fundamental principles of quantitative and qualitative research, and familiarises students with commonly used communication research methods. The focus is on developing a deeper understanding of the research process through a 'learning by doing' approach.

learning_outcomes.description: Understand fundamental principles of quantitative research.
Understand fundamental principles of qualitative research.
Understand the fundamental principles relating to ethical conduct of research.
Become familiar with research methodologies commonly used to investigate communication behaviour: surveys; experiments; content analysis; interviews; ethnographic observation.
Use research to find answers to real world problems.
Design a research project that addresses a specific research question.
Work collaboratively to communicate research findings.
Communicate about research to professional and lay audiences.

assessment_summary.title: Tutorial Presentation
assessment_summary.due_date: This assessment will take place in students regularly assigned tutorials from Week 5 to Week 12. Students will be assigned their presentation groups and presentation week at the beginning of the semester.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial Presentation
assessment_details.due_date: This assessment will take place in students regularly assigned tutorials from Week 5 to Week 12. Students will be assigned their presentation groups and presentation week at the beginning of the semester.
assessment_details.task_description: Students will form small groups to deliver a short presentation and facilitate a class discussion relating to a course concept. Groups will be required to draw on course material and conduct their own research to inform their presentation and discussion. Tutorial presentations will happen at the beginning of tutorials and will last for approximately 15-20 minutes. Each group will be assigned a presentation week at the beginning of semester. This is a hurdle assessment and must be attempted to pass the course. If you fail to complete the tutorial presentation hurdle you will receive a (maximum) final grade of 3 for the entire course, regardless of performance in other assessment items. Recording of Oral and Practical Assessment All presentation s will be recorded for marking purposes via recording facilities available where the assessment takes place (eg. ECHO360, Zoom, camera device) Recordings will be retained by the School of Communication and Arts for at least 12 months from the release of the final grade for the course. Recordings will be stored in a secure manner and will only be accessed by authorised school staff for the purposes of: Moderation of marking; Provision of feedback to the student(s) recorded; and/or Re-marking following a successful re-mark application. This assessment task is to be completed in-person. The use of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
This assessment will take place in students regularly assigned tutorials. After completing the oral presentation, students will be required to upload their supplementary materials (i.e. power point files) to Black Board.
Deferral or extension
You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
If exceptional circumstances apply and a student is unable to be present for their presentation they may contact the course coordinator with appropriate documentation to arrange a new presentation date.
Late submission
Given that this is in-person group presentation there are no options for late submissions unless exceptional circumstances apply. All group members are required to attend and contribute to the presentation. If a group member is absent, the present group members will be instructed to continue the presentation and be graded as usual. The missing group member will receive an automatic 0 unless exceptional circumstances apply or their absence has been communicated and approved ahead of time by the course coordinator.

assessment_details.title: Individual Ethics Reflection
assessment_details.task_description: The first written assessment is a reflective essay that requires students to critically consider the importance of ethics in research design. Students will be asked to consider the varied ethical challenges raised by up to two methods covered in weeks 1-5 of the course. Using previous studies, students will need to discuss the implications of these challenges and make suggestions for how they could be overcome in future research. Individual feedback from assessment will help guide assessment 3, the individual research proposal. This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
Submit via TurnItIn on Blackboard.
TurnItIn Receipts:
Assignments for this course will be submitted electronically via Blackboard and using TurnItIn . Before submitting any assignments for this course you must ensure you have completed UQ's compulsory online Academic Integrity Tutorial.
When you successfully submit your assessment to TurnItIn you will see text confirming your submission is complete, before being redirected to your Assignment inbox. On this page you can:
View the name of the submitted file View date and time of the upload Resubmit your paper (if necessary) Download your submitted paper Download digital receipt.
If you cannot see your submission in your Assignment inbox you should regard your submission as unsuccessful. Students are responsible for retaining evidence of submission by the due date for all assessment items, in the required form (e.g. screenshot, email, photo, and an unaltered copy of submitted work).
If the submission was not successful:
Note the error message (preferably take a screenshot). Go to your assignment page and see if it is possible to submit again. If you cannot submit again email your course coordinator immediately with the assignment attached.
Please visit this webpage for further advice on how to submit your TurnItIn assignment .
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 28 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

assessment_details.title: Research Proposal
assessment_details.task_description: The final assessment will require students to write a formal, individual research proposal. The proposal will require students to develop a problem statement and set of research questions. Students will need to outline: A clear methodological approach and rationale Ethical problems that may arise from their proposed project The scale and feasibility of the proposed research This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI and/or MT technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which artificial intelligence will provide only limited support and guidance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. To pass this assessment, students will be required to demonstrate detailed comprehension of their written submission independent of AI and MT tools. learning_activities.topic: Week 1 Lecture: Introduction to the course and the research process Learning outcomes: L01
Week 1: NO TUTORIALS Tutorials start in Week 2
Week 2 Lecture: Fundamental principles: Quantitative and qualitative approaches to research and research ethics Learning outcomes: L01, L03

Week 2 Tutorial: The tutorials will be conducted as workshops that include exercises designed to increase understanding and application of lecture content as well as aide with completion of assessment. Group work is an essential aspect of the course, so attending tutorials to work with your group is essential. Groups for upcoming assessment will be formed in this tutorial. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L09
Week 3 Lecture: Fundamental principles: Sampling Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L06
Week 3 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L06, L07, L09
Week 4 Lecture: Fundamental principles: measurement Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L06
Week 4 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L04, L06, L07, L09
Week 5 Lecture: Quantitative methods: surveys and experiments Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05, L06
Week 5 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05, L06, L07, L09
Week 6 Lecture: Quantitative methods: content analysis Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05, L06
Week 6: NO TUTORIAL No tutorials due to Public Holiday. Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05, L06, L07, L09
MID-SEMESTER BREAK
Week 7 Lecture: Basic quantitative analysis Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L06, L08
Week 7 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L06, L08, L09
Week 8: Lecture Qualitative methods Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L07, L08
Week 8 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L05, L06, L08, L09
Week 9 Lecture: Qualitative methods: interviews and focus groups Learning outcomes: L02, L05, L06, L08
Week 9 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L02, L05, L06, L08, L09
Week 10 Lecture: Qualitative methods - ethnography and participant observation Learning outcomes: L02, L05, L06, L08
Week 10 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L02, L05, L06, L08, L09
Week 11 Lecture: Qualitative analysis Learning outcomes: L02, L06, L08
Week 11 Tutorial: Tutorial exercises, assessment guidance, group work Learning outcomes: L02, L06, L08, L09
Week 12 Lecture: Future challenges for communication researchers Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L06
Week 12 Tutorial: Final feedback and guidance about assessment with tutors Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L09
Week 13: NO LECTURES
Week 13: NO TUTORIALS

policies_and_procedures: University policies and procedures apply to all aspects of student life. As a UQ student, you must comply with University-wide and program-specific requirements, including the: Student Code of Conduct Policy Student Integrity and Misconduct Policy and Procedure Assessment Procedure Examinations Procedure Reasonable Adjustments for Students Policy and Procedure AI for Assessment Guide Recording of Teaching Policy and Procedure Learn more about UQ policies on my.UQ and the Policy and Procedure Library . Course guidelines Communication Expectations While you are a student at UQ, all communication must be conducted according to the UQ Student Code of Conduct . The UQ Library has a helpful Communicate and collaborate online module . Email is the primary way for you to send messages to, and receive information from, the School and our staff. You must use your UQ email address (not a private address) to communicate with staff. You should add a clear subject line, including course code, and a 2-3 word statement. You can send email at any time, however please do not expect responses outside normal working hours (Monday to Friday from ~8am to ~5pm). Emails that constitute bullying, harassment or discrimination against staff contravene the Student Code of Conduct. Emails like this will be reported to the University, and the matter will be pursued as misconduct. Learning activities
